title: Partial Entropy production of active particles with hidden states in potentials
abstract: Partially observed stochastic systems can appear (almost) time-reversal symmetric while in fact operating far from equilibrium. The present work extends the perturbative framework introduced in [Phys. Rev. Lett. 136, 198302 (2026)] to calculate in a generic confining potential the partial entropy production, which quantifies the time-reversal asymmetry of a generic active particle with hidden self-propulsion. Focusing on the harmonic case, we apply our framework to reproduce an exact result for the partial entropy production rate of an active Ornstein-Uhlenbeck particle and to derive the partial entropy production rate of a run-and-tumble particle.
summary: This theoretical paper develops a framework to calculate the partial entropy production rate for active particles whose self-propulsion is hidden. Using advanced path integral and field-theoretic methods, the authors derive explicit, high-order expressions for this rate for models like the RnT particle. The results show how the partial observation of the system fundamentally alters the apparent time-reversal symmetry breaking compared to the full description.
main_problem: The paper quantifies the partial entropy production rate ($\dot{S}_x$) for active particles whose self-propulsion mechanism is hidden from observation, addressing how partial observation can mask non-equilibrium dynamics.
main_result: The partial EPR ($\dot{S}_x$) is derived through complex path integral expansions, yielding results that depend on the correlation functions of the hidden self-propulsion noise $w(t)$ and the confining potential $V(x)$.
method: The methodology involves extending perturbative path integral frameworks, utilizing cumulant expansions, and employing Doi-Peliti field theory techniques to calculate time-ordered correlation functions.
model_or_system: The system models overdamped active particles governed by a Langevin equation incorporating a confining potential $V(x)$ and a stochastic self-propulsion term $w(t)$. Specific models analyzed include the Active Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Particle (AOUP) and the Run-and-Tumble (RnT) particle.
key_observables: Partial Entropy Production Rate ($\dot{S}_x$), Full Entropy Production Rate ($\dot{S}_{x,w}$), and various time-ordered correlation functions (up to six-time).
important_parameters:
Self-propulsion strength ($
u$), diffusion coefficient ($D$), and the parameters defining the confining potential ($k$ for harmonic potential).
main_assumptions:
The analysis assumes the existence of a unique steady state and relies on approximations such as leading order in $
u$ and the use of the Stratonovich convention.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the comparison of full vs. partial EPR for RnT processes in free and harmonic cases, showing how hidden states change the observable asymmetry.
paper_structure: The paper builds from the general formalism of partial EPR using path probability ratios, applies this to specific models (AOUP, RnT), and details the calculation using advanced field-theoretic diagrammatic expansions for higher-order correlation functions.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous, field-theoretic approach to quantifying non-equilibrium thermodynamics in active matter, which is crucial for understanding energy dissipation and irreversible processes in complex biological or synthetic systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Run-and-tumble (RnT) process with (i) fully resolved (red corresponding to self-propulsion towards the right, blue to self-propulsion to the left) and (ii) hidden self-propulsion velocity, in the left and right columns respectively. Rows cor- respond to a: a) free, and b) harmonically confined, RnT par- ticle. In a), trajectories break time-reversal symmetry (TRS) when fully resolved, as revealed by the effective swapping of colours under mirroring along the vertical axis. They obey TRS when the self-propulsion state is hidden. The process therefore has positive total EPR ˙Sx,w > 0 and zero partial EPR ˙Sx = 0. In b), trajectories break TRS even with hidden self-propulsion,...
